hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Primary adoption risk: users still drop during local setup and credential configuration due to environment-specific errors, so the improved quickstart completion should not be treated as end-to-end onboarding success until credential setup has a recovery path.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly says the biggest remaining drop is local setup/credential configuration with environment-specific errors and warns not to present the improved completion metric as an end-to-end onboarding win until credential setup has its own recovery path.

claimJudgments.claimText: Operational/support risk: many new tickets are driven by environment drift, stale CLI versions, and unclear recovery steps; an internal checklist resolves most cases but isn’t in public docs, leading to repeated failed states and reopened tickets.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says most new tickets mention environment drift, stale CLI versions, and unclear recovery steps; the internal checklist resolved most cases but is not reflected in public docs; and several tickets reopened when users repeated the quickstart and reproduced the failed state.

claimJudgments.claimText: Pilot/launch risk: the most visible failure in a demo is setup recovery; expansion is recommended only after failed-install guidance and citation checks are observable in the lab, so the experience feels calm in front of observers.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 identifies setup recovery as the visible failure that would make the demo feel brittle, recommends expansion only after failed-install guidance and citation checks are observable in the lab, and states the owner wants the next pilot to feel calm in front of observers.

claimJudgments.claimText: Trust/decision-use risk: senior engineers only trust generated recommendations when each claim links back to inspectable source snippets and when provenance (analytics vs support vs interviews) is clearly separated, not blended.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly supports this: senior engineers trust recommendations only when claims point back to inspectable source snippets, and the strongest trust signal was separating analytics, support tickets, and interview evidence rather than blending them.

claimJudgments.claimText: Approval boundary (gates to add teams): scheduling is blocked until the failed-install path has public recovery steps and can be demonstrated without support pasting an internal checklist; additional gates require source citations and provenance visibility in the lab.
claimJudgments.explanation: S6 lists the gates for adding teams: public failed-install recovery steps, source citations, and provenance visibility. It also says scheduling is blocked until setup recovery can be demonstrated without support pasting an internal checklist.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation directly addresses the user task by summarizing adoption risks and prioritizing the next product-quality improvement: a self-serve setup/failed-install recovery path before pilot expansion, with citation/provenance readiness included. This is well supported by S1, S2, S4, and S6, with no material overreach.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
